dust 0.8.7

Dust Random Number Generator

Source: R/rng.R
dust_rng.Rd

Create an object that can be used to generate random numbers with the same RNG as dust uses internally. This is primarily meant for debugging and testing.

Methods

Public methods

  • dust_rng$new()

  • dust_rng$size()

  • dust_rng$jump()

  • dust_rng$long_jump()

  • dust_rng$unif_rand()

  • dust_rng$norm_rand()

  • dust_rng$runif()

  • dust_rng$rnorm()

  • dust_rng$rbinom()

  • dust_rng$rpois()

  • dust_rng$rexp()

  • dust_rng$state()

Method new()

Create a dust_rng object

Usage

dust_rng$new(seed, n_generators = 1L)

Arguments

seed

The seed, as an integer or as a raw vector.

n_generators

The number of generators to use. While this function never runs in parallel, this is used to create a set of interleaved independent generators as dust would use in a model.

Method size()

Number of generators available

Usage

dust_rng$size()

Method jump()

The jump function for the generator, equivalent to 2^128 numbers drawn from the generator.

Usage

dust_rng$jump()

Method long_jump()

The long_jump function for the generator, equivalent to 2^192 numbers drawn from the generator. Generate n numbers from a standard uniform distribution

Usage

dust_rng$long_jump()

Method unif_rand()

Usage

dust_rng$unif_rand(n)

Arguments

n

Number of samples to draw Generate n numbers from a standard normal distribution

Method norm_rand()

Usage

dust_rng$norm_rand(n)

Arguments

n

Number of samples to draw Generate n numbers from a uniform distribution

Method runif()

Usage

dust_rng$runif(n, min, max)

Arguments

n

Number of samples to draw

min

The minimum of the distribution (length 1 or n)

max

The maximum of the distribution (length 1 or n) Generate n numbers from a normal distribution

Method rnorm()

Usage

dust_rng$rnorm(n, mean, sd)

Arguments

n

Number of samples to draw

mean

The mean of the distribution (length 1 or n)

sd

The standard deviation of the distribution (length 1 or n) Generate n numbers from a binomial distribution

Method rbinom()

Usage

dust_rng$rbinom(n, size, prob)

Arguments

n

Number of samples to draw

size

The number of trials (zero or more, length 1 or n)

prob

The probability of success on each trial (between 0 and 1, length 1 or n) Generate n numbers from a Poisson distribution

Method rpois()

Usage

dust_rng$rpois(n, lambda)

Arguments

n

Number of samples to draw

lambda

The mean (zero or more, length 1 or n) Generate n numbers from a exponential distribution

Method rexp()

Usage

dust_rng$rexp(n, rate)

Arguments

n

Number of samples to draw

rate

The rate of the exponential

Method state()

Returns the state of the random number generator. This returns a raw vector of length 32 * n_generators. It is primarily intended for debugging as one cannot (yet) initialise a dust_rng object with this state.

Usage

dust_rng$state()

Examples

rng <- dust_rng$new(42)

# Shorthand for Uniform(0, 1)
rng$unif_rand(5)

#> [1] 0.3614713 0.8809038 0.6228497 0.9013818 0.1483888

# Shorthand for Normal(0, 1)
rng$norm_rand(5)

#> [1] -0.8431672  0.6774323 -0.3107898  0.1477770  0.7396579

# Uniform random numbers between min and max
rng$runif(5, -2, 6)

#> [1]  2.607481  4.946376 -1.320962  4.695593  2.196486

# Normally distributed random numbers with mean and sd
rng$rnorm(5, 4, 2)

#> [1] 0.9321614 6.1411322 6.1099544 4.9142178 5.1787420

# Binomially distributed random numbers with size and prob
rng$rbinom(5, 10L, 0.3)

#> [1] 5 3 2 1 3

# Poisson distributed random numbers with mean lambda
rng$rpois(5, 2)

#> [1] 2 2 1 3 3

Contents
